summary: MailPoet has a well-architected security model using Doctrine ORM and layered access controls. No unauthenticated RCE, SQLi, or object injection was found. Key findings are an unauthenticated info disclosure endpoint, an unauthenticated ActionScheduler flush enabling DoS, a weak cron daemon token, and a stored XSS via display_name shortcode. Most automated 'critical RCE' findings were false positives (array_map with closures/intval).

findings.title: Unauthenticated Site Reconnaissance via Analytics API
findings.description: The Analytics endpoint has NO_ACCESS_RESTRICTION permissions, allowing any unauthenticated user to retrieve site fingerprint data including WordPress version, active theme name+version, WooCommerce version, sending method (PHPMail/SendGrid/SES), subscriber count, MailPoet installation date, and whether Gutenberg is installed.
attack_chain: POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php with action=mailpoet_token to get a valid nonce (unauthenticated)
Receive full site fingerprint: WP version, theme+version, WooCommerce version, sending method, subscriber count
findings.poc: curl -s -X POST 'http://TARGET/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' --data 'action=mailpoet_token' | jq -r '.data.token' | xargs -I{} curl -s -X POST 'http://TARGET/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php' --data 'action=mailpoet&api_version=v1&endpoint=analytics&method=get_tracking_data&token={}'
findings.remediation: Change Analytics endpoint permissions to require PERMISSION_MANAGE_SETTINGS or remove the public endpoint entirely. The tracking data is only useful for MailPoet's own telemetry and should not be publicly accessible.

findings.title: Unauthenticated ActionScheduler Queue Flush (DoS)
findings.description: The wp_ajax_nopriv_mailpoet-cron-action-scheduler-run handler has zero authentication -- no nonce, no token, no IP check. Any unauthenticated user can trigger full ActionScheduler queue processing including email sending, bounce processing, subscriber cleanup, and WooCommerce sync. MailPoet also overrides ActionScheduler's concurrent batch limit (ensureConcurrency forces min 2), removing the one internal guard against concurrent runners. Repeated requests can cause CPU/memory exhaustion and MySQL connection pool saturation.
attack_chain: Repeated requests spawn multiple concurrent runners, exhausting server resources
findings.remediation: Add a shared secret (HMAC token) to the triggerExecutor() POST body and verify it in runActionScheduler(). Alternatively restrict to loopback IP (127.0.0.1/::1) since the endpoint is designed for self-triggered requests.

findings.title: Weak Cron Daemon Token (25.8 bits entropy, brute-forceable)
findings.description: The cron daemon token protecting the /?mailpoet_router&endpoint=cron_daemon&action=run route is generated by Security::generateRandomString() with default length of 5 characters from base-36 charset (a-z0-9). This yields only 36^5 = 60.5 million possibilities (~25.8 bits of entropy). The endpoint has NO_ACCESS_RESTRICTION and no rate limiting or lockout on failed attempts. At 1000 req/s the full keyspace is exhaustible in ~17 hours. A valid token grants ability to trigger the full cron daemon, running all MailPoet workers (email sending, subscriber management, cleanup, sync).
findings.remediation: Increase default token length from 5 to at least 32 characters in CronHelper::createToken(). Add rate limiting or lockout on the cron daemon run endpoint. The Security::generateRandomString() minimum should be raised from 5 to at least 20.

findings.title: Stored XSS via [subscriber:displayname] Shortcode in View-in-Browser
findings.description: The [subscriber:displayname] shortcode returns the WP user's display_name without HTML escaping, unlike [subscriber:firstname] and [subscriber:lastname] which use htmlspecialchars(). WordPress allows arbitrary HTML in display_name. A WP user with a malicious display_name who is also a MailPoet subscriber can trigger XSS in the view-in-browser rendering. The output chain (ViewInBrowserRenderer -> ViewInBrowser endpoint -> raw echo) applies no escaping. However, exploitation requires: (1) an authenticated WP user account, (2) a newsletter using [subscriber:displayname], (3) social engineering a victim to visit the attacker's view-in-browser URL (which contains a subscriber_token specific to the attacker).
findings.remediation: Apply htmlspecialchars($wpUser->display_name, ENT_QUOTES | ENT_SUBSTITUTE | ENT_HTML401) at Subscriber.php line 62, matching the pattern used for firstname/lastname.

findings.title: Base64 Image Write to Public Directory (Authenticated, Editor+)
findings.description: An authenticated user with mailpoet_manage_emails capability (Editor+) can save newsletter templates with arbitrary base64 data in the thumbnail_data field. The outer check validates data:image/jpeg;base64 prefix but the inner saveBase64AsImageFile() uses a looser regex (data:.*;base64). Files are written to wp-content/uploads/mailpoet/newsletter_thumbnails/ which lacks .htaccess protection (unlike the exports/ directory). On misconfigured Apache servers, a JPEG-PHP polyglot could achieve code execution. The file extension is always .jpg, significantly limiting practical exploitation.
findings.remediation: Tighten the inner regex to match only data:image/jpeg;base64. Add .htaccess deny-all and index.php to the newsletter_thumbnails directory. Validate JPEG magic bytes post-decode.

findings.title: Unauthenticated Nonce Vending Nullifies CSRF Protection
findings.description: The wp_ajax_nopriv_mailpoet_token endpoint freely vends valid WordPress nonces to any unauthenticated visitor. This is intentional (for edge-cached signup forms) but means the nonce provides no CSRF protection for any MailPoet endpoint. All security relies on the permission_callback layer, which is properly implemented. This is a design observation, not a directly exploitable vulnerability.

false_positives.reason: array_map with a closure (not a string callback to exec/system). The callback is an inline function doing floatval/intval type casting. This is the documented FP pattern: array_map('intval') and array_map(function(){}) are NOT RCE.
array_map with a closure doing intval() type casting on segment options. Not RCE.
array_map('intval', $result) applied to wpdb->get_col() output. This is the exact documented FP pattern. wpdb->prepare is used correctly with parameterized placeholders.
array_map('intval', $result) on wpdb->get_col() output. Prepared statement with %s placeholders. Standard FP pattern.
array_map with closure doing type casting on wpdb query results. Static SQL query with no user input. Not RCE.
intval() cast followed by Doctrine ORM query. No shell execution. Not RCE.
array_map with closure calling fromArray() static constructor on wpdb results. Prepared statement with %i and %d. Not RCE.

The $_GET['mailpoet_form_iframe'] value is cast to (int) before use: (int)$_GET['mailpoet_form_iframe']. Integer cast completely prevents XSS. The form content loaded by ID is admin-defined. The is_numeric() check plus integer cast makes injection impossible.

get_option('blog_charset') is a server-side WordPress setting, not user-controllable input. An attacker cannot influence the blog_charset option without admin access. The Content-Type header injection requires controlling the charset value, which is impossible without prior admin compromise.

getenv('MAILPOET_TRACY_LOG_DIR') reads a server environment variable, not user input. Environment variables are set by the server administrator, not by HTTP requests. This is a server configuration issue, not an exploitable vulnerability.

areas_reviewed: All v1 JSON API endpoints (lib/API/JSON/v1/*.php) - permission checks
All REST API endpoints - permission callbacks
Unserialize calls in vendor-prefixed libraries and first-party code
Doctrine entity column type mappings (ArrayType, SerializedArrayType, JsonOrSerializedType)
Newsletter template rendering and shortcode system
File upload paths: ThumbnailSaver, TemplateImageLoader, ExportDownload
not_found: Unauthenticated RCE - No eval(), exec(), system(), or command injection found reachable from unauthenticated context
SQL injection - Doctrine ORM + consistent wpdb->prepare() usage. Sort parameters sanitized via strspn() allowlist
Object injection - All unserialize calls operate on Doctrine-managed serialized arrays, not user input. Write paths go through sanitizers before Doctrine serializes
Unauthenticated file upload - All upload/import endpoints require Editor+ capabilities
SSRF - Template image loader uses strict URL allowlist; MailChimp import constrains hostnames
